data.source: Laboratory experiments using identity salience priming and incentivized choice tasks
data.wave_or_subset: Experiment 1 (ethnicity salience among Asian-American and white students); Experiment 2 (race and gender salience at University of Michigan and Temple University); subset analyses excluding subjects skeptical about delayed-payment credibility
data.sample_size: Experiment 1: 71 Asian-Americans and 66 whites; Experiment 2: regression sample sizes ranged from 106 to 444; believer-only subset sample sizes ranged from 56 to 374
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual participant (pooled choices per subject for interval regression)
data.notes: Time preference measured via 46 binary intertemporal choices; risk preference via 18 binary risky choices. Immediate payments by check; delayed payments via post-dated checks. A substantial fraction of subjects expressed skepticism about delayed-payment credibility; analyses also reported for believers-only subset. Sites included Harvard (ethnicity salience context noted), University of Michigan, and Temple University.

method.description: The study used psychological priming to make social identities (ethnicity, race, gender) salient and measured resulting changes in intertemporal and risk preferences. Participants completed incentivized binary choice tasks over time and risk. The authors estimated interval regressions with latent outcomes defined as log reservation interest rates for time preferences and reservation risk premia for risk preferences, comparing primed vs. control conditions within demographic groups and across sites.
steps: Recruit undergraduate participants (Experiment 1: Asian-American and white students; Experiment 2: native black, immigrant black, and white students; men and women) at university labs (Harvard, University of Michigan, Temple).
Administer identity-salience primes (e.g., questions about ethnic background, race, or gender) or control prompts.
Elicit time preferences via 46 binary choices between smaller-sooner and larger-later payments (half immediate vs. one week later; half one week vs. two weeks later).
Elicit risk preferences via 18 binary choices between a sure $4 and a lottery paying $8 with varying probabilities (25% to 76%).
Pay participants by immediate check for sooner payments and via post-dated checks for delayed payments.
Compute each subject’s reservation values: minimum continuously compounded weekly interest rate to choose the later payment, and minimum risk premium to accept the gamble.

Estimate interval regressions for log reservation interest rate and reservation risk premium, comparing identity-salience treatments to controls within demographic groups, including site and task covariates.
Conduct robustness checks including analyses excluding participants skeptical about the credibility of delayed payments.
method.models: Interval regression models for latent outcomes: log of the minimum continuously compounded weekly interest rate (time preferences) and log/minimum risk premium (risk preferences), with treatment indicators for identity salience and covariates (task features and site).
method.outcome_variable: Log reservation weekly interest rate (time preferences); reservation risk premium (risk preferences); percent impatient/safe choices (descriptive).
method.independent_variables: Identity-salience treatment indicators (ethnicity, race, gender); demographic group indicators (Asian-American, white, native black, immigrant black, male, female); task features (immediate vs. delayed horizon; larger stakes); site indicators (University of Michigan, Temple).
method.control_variables: Task horizon (1 week vs. 2 weeks); stakes size; site (UMich/Temple); potentially interactions by demographic group as reported in tables.

results.summary: Making social identity salient affected economic preferences in specific groups. Ethnicity salience made Asian-American participants more patient, substantially reducing their reservation interest rates for delayed payments, with no effect on risk premiums for Asians or whites. Race salience reduced reservation interest rates (increased patience) among native blacks, with stronger or significant effects in some samples and sites, and increased risk aversion among native blacks, particularly in believers-only analyses. Gender salience had no significant effects on patience or risk preferences. Payment-credibility skepticism affected sample composition, but believers-only analyses showed qualitatively similar or stronger effects in some cases.

numerical_results.outcome_name: Percent impatient choices (Asians) under ethnicity salience vs. control
Log reservation interest rate (Asians): ethnicity-salient treatment coefficient
Reservation interest rate (Asians), $4 now vs. later: primed vs. control
